texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The process for separating and purifying lithium in brines based on electrodialysis with monovalent selective ion exchange membranes was investigated in our previous work. The migration of coexisting cations in brines was competitive with lithium ions, especially monovalent cations (Na + and K + ). The aim of this study was to examine the in fl uence of major coexisting cations in brines on lithium recovery in the selective-electrodialysis (SED) process. Considering the factors of coexisting cations, such as concentration and type, some migration laws of lithium ion were found as follows: the concentration of coexisting cations had negative e ff ect on the migration of lithium ion; the in fl uence order of coexisting cations on lithium migration was contrary to their hydrated radius sequence: K + >Na + >Ca 2+ > Mg 2+. In order to characterize the migration process of cations through monovalent selective cation exchange membrane in microcosmic theory, a partial dehydration conceptual model based on charge capillary column theory and ionic potential was proposed. And the model was used to characterize the ions migration process. Simultaneously, considering the hydration potential which indicates how strongly an ion would attract water molecules, the in fl uence sequence of coexisting cations was explained legitimately. These observations might provide some theoretical basis and technological support for the relevant research of recovering lithium from brines.

And a wide range of salt lake brines containing lithium has been exploited by humanity in precipitation method. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electrodialysis (ED) is an electric ion exchange membrane technology that has some signi fi cant superiority in seawater desalination, extraction of metal resources in salt lake brines, and treatment of industrial e ffl uents, and so on [22 -24]. Due to its superior desalination performance and high-e ffi cient enrichment of high concentration of salt solution, ED as a potential e ff ective method is used in concentrated brines [25]. ED is based on the transport of ions through ion exchange membranes under an electrical potential di ff erence as the driving force of mass transfer. When the conventional ion exchange membranes are replaced by monovalent selective ones partially, a new type of electrodialysis, called selectrodialysis (SED), is formed [26]. However, the con fi guration of membrane stack in this manuscript is that all of the standard anion- and cation exchange membranes are replaced with the monovalent selective anion- and cation exchange membranes. In order to di ff erentiate, the membrane stack with above con fi guration was renamed as ' selective-electrodialysis (S-ED) ' in this work. The principle of the S-ED stack is shown in Fig. 1.
texts.text: Electrodialysis (ED) is an electric ion exchange membrane technology that has some signi fi cant superiority in seawater desalination, extraction of metal resources in salt lake brines, and treatment of industrial e ffl uents, and so on [22 -24]. Due to its superior desalination performance and high-e ffi cient enrichment of high concentration of salt solution, ED as a potential e ff ective method is used in concentrated brines [25]. ED is based on the transport of ions through ion exchange membranes under an electrical potential di ff erence as the driving force of mass transfer. When the conventional ion exchange membranes are replaced by monovalent selective ones partially, a new type of electrodialysis, called selectrodialysis (SED), is formed [26]. However, the con fi guration of membrane stack in this manuscript is that all of the standard anion- and cation exchange membranes are replaced with the monovalent selective anion- and cation exchange membranes. In order to di ff erentiate, the membrane stack with above con fi guration was renamed as ' selective-electrodialysis (S-ED) ' in this work. The principle of the S-ED stack is shown in Fig. 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Monovalent selective cation exchange membranes and monovalent selective anion exchange membranes are placed alternately in S-ED stack. When a voltage is applied between the cathode and the anode, cations migrate toward the cathode side and anions toward anode side. So the monovalent cations (Li + , Na + , K + ) migrate through cation exchange membranes which have negative fi xed groups, and are retained by the anion exchange membranes which have positive active groups. And the divalent cations (Ca 2+ , Mg 2+ ) are blocked by monovalent selective cation exchange membranes and stranded in desalting compartment. This migration process causes an increase of the monovalent ion concentration in concentrating compartments and a decrease in the desalting compartments.
texts.text: Monovalent selective cation exchange membranes and monovalent selective anion exchange membranes are placed alternately in S-ED stack. When a voltage is applied between the cathode and the anode, cations migrate toward the cathode side and anions toward anode side. So the monovalent cations (Li + , Na + , K + ) migrate through cation exchange membranes which have negative fi xed groups, and are retained by the anion exchange membranes which have positive active groups. And the divalent cations (Ca 2+ , Mg 2+ ) are blocked by monovalent selective cation exchange membranes and stranded in desalting compartment. This migration process causes an increase of the monovalent ion concentration in concentrating compartments and a decrease in the desalting compartments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nowadays, there is little information about recovering lithium from brines by ED using monovalent selective ion exchange membrane. Nie et al. [27] investigated the fractionation of lithium ions from magnesium ions by electrodialysis, and the results illustrated that ED exhibited superiority both technically and economically for the fractionation of Li + /Mg 2+ compared to NF. Lithium recovery from high Mg 2+ /Li + brines by S-ED process was also investigated preliminarily in our previous work, and the separation of Mg 2+ and Li + was partly solved [28]. But, the coexisting cations should be a certain negative in fl uence on the migration and puri fi cation of lithium in theory. And those coexisting monovalent cations (Na + and K + ) would generate obvious competitive permeation along with Li + migration in ED process. This is also the reason why the current e ffi ciency in the process of recovering lithium from brines by S-ED technology usually is lower. Furthermore, although the monovalent selectivity cation exchange membrane has lower selectivity for magnesium and calcium, and the separation e ff ect of lithium and magnesium/calcium by S-ED process is obvious prior to nano fi ltration (NF) process, the separation e ffi ciency of lithium and magnesium/calcium should still be further improved. Also, those coexisting divalent cations (Ca 2+ and Mg 2+ ) still have a certain impact on the migration of Li + . Thus, the in fl uence of coexisting cations (Na + , K + , Ca 2+ and Mg 2+ ) in brines on lithium migration by batch S-ED process was studied in this work, in hoping of providing technical support for the comprehensive utilization of lithium resource in salt lake brines, seawater and bittern.

texts.orig: The batch S-ED set-up schematized in Fig. 2 was provided by Beijing Huanyulida Environmental Products Ltd, China. The desalting, concentrating and electrolyte circulate solutions were in three closed loop, respectively. The experimental S-ED apparatus mainly contained the following parts: (1) a direct current (DC) power supply (JS3020D, Wuxi ANS Electronic Technology Ltd., China); (2) a cathode and an anode, which were made of titanium coated with ruthenium; (3) S-ED membrane stack (30 cm × 10 cm); (4) three solution storage tanks (desalting solution tank, concentrating solution tank and electrolyte solution tank); (5) three magnetic pumps (DP-100, Shanghai Xinxishan Industrial Ltd., China).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our preliminary work, the optimized operation conditions of recovering lithium from brines in this S-ED device were obtained: the applied voltage (5 V), the linear velocities of desalting/concentrating/ electrolyte (6.2, 6.2 and 3.8 cm/s) [28]. Meanwhile, this S-ED setup under 5 V did work below the limit current in all the experiments. So, the experimental operation conditions in this work were based on above-mentioned results. And, it is necessary to circulate 15 min for eliminating the gas bubbles inside the S-ED stack before the experiments. It can prevent gathering the hot of gas bubbles in S-ED stack [29], which could cause to reduce the current e ffi ciency of S-ED stack and damage the ion-exchange membranes. Then the magnetic pumps conveyed these solutions into the respective compartments. All experiments were run for 120 min, and samples were taken every 20 min.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane stack was consisted of eleven cation exchange membranes (CIMS) and ten anion exchange membranes (ACS). So the membrane stack contained ten cell pairs and two electrode compartments. Furthermore, an extra CIMS was placed adjacent to electrode, which is aimed to intercept the migration of Cl -to anode compartment to avoid producing chlorine. On the other hand, cation exchange membrane shows a better stability in S-ED process, and it is di ffi cult to su ff er damage from the in fl uence of electrode reaction. The e ff ective area of each membrane was 140.7 cm 2 . The CIMS and ACS were isolated by plastic sheet mesh spacers with 0.90 mm thickness.

The curves of migration rate and recovery ratio of Li + are shown in Fig. 4. It can be seen in Fig. 4(a) that the migration rate of Li + is decreased with operation time, and the descending rate are increased with the adding Na + up when Na + /Li + ratios are 1, 3 and 5. The Mr value of Li + decreases from 10.12 mmol/ (m 2 min) to 2.41 mmol/(m 2 min) when Na + /Li + mole ratio is from 1 to 30. This was mainly because ions content was low in desalting compartment and S-ED system lacked enough ions to remove after a certain time, when the value of Na + /Li + ratio was lower. On the other hand, the time of lithium migration extended with the increasing of ionic strength, leading to the stabilization of its rate with time when the Na + /Li + ratio is 10 -30. However, the migration rate of Li + shows a clear downward trend with the increase of Na + /Li + ratio. The variation of recovery ratio of Li + with time under di ff erent Na + /Li + ratios is shown in Fig. 4(b). It illustrates the RLi decreases with the rise of Na + / Li + in those experiments. When the Na + /Li + ratio is 1 and 3, the RLi increases within 60 min linearly, and then the growth becomes slow. And the values could achieve to 74.31% and 76.03%, respectively. This was due to the declining of lithium migration rate in desalting compartment, which was caused by decrease of current density at the fi nal stage of the batch S-ED process. Furthermore, the RLi is close to each other when the Na + /Li + ratio is 10 and 15, and a tiny e ff ect of adding Na + on RLi appears in the range of 10 -15. Moreover, the RLi value maintains at a lower level when the Na + /Li + ratio is 20 or 30. This is because the migration of lithium is restrained by abundant sodium which holds the great mass of S-ED current.

texts.orig: Fig. 5 shows the variation of FNa -Li with time at di ff erent Na + /Li + ratios. It can be seen in Fig. 5(a) that the FNa -Li value is less than 1 except for Na + /Li + = 1 and 3. According to the de fi nition of separation coe ffi cient, it implies a disadvantage to the separation of lithium and sodium when the FNa -Li is lower than 1. Therefore, higher additive amount of sodium induces worse separating e ff ect. On the other hand, the FNa -Li >1 when the Na + /Li + ratio is 1 or 3. It is incorrect in theory because both lithium and sodium, which have minor hydrated radius, are easy to pass through the monovalent selective cation exchange membrane. And both of them should be migrated completely under a weak ionic strength. The reasons of this phenomenon are as follows: The total ionic amount in desalting compartment (0.05 mol/L NaCl, 0.15 mol/L NaCl) is excessive lower than concentrating compartment (0.5 mol/L NaCl) in the above experiments. This results in concentration di ff usion phenomenon forming between concentrating and desalting compartments, i.e. sodium in concentrating compartment spreads reversely to desalting compartment. Then, the concentration of sodium in desalting compartment is apparently higher than the actual value of electro-migration. A trace of lithium in desalting compartment

texts.orig: The variation of current e ffi ciency ( η ) and speci fi c energy consumption (ESEC) with time at di ff erent Na + /Li + ratios are shown in Fig. 6. It can be seen from Fig. 6(a) that the current e ffi ciency increase fi rstly and then gradual decline at Na + /Li + = 1, and the maximum of η is 41.54% after 60 min. This is mainly due to the higher content of lithium than sodium in a short time. Moreover, current e ffi ciency of other ratios is approximately constant with time. And it decreases in di ff erent gradient with the rising addition of coexisting sodium ions. The current e ffi ciency is less than 10% at Na + /Li + ≥ 5. Moreover, the value of η is less than 5% when Na + /Li + ratio is 20 or 30, which means both are disadvantageous obviously to lithium migration. This is because that sodium ion possesses a lower hydrated radius and the competitive e ff ect of sodium has been remarkably strengthened with the increase of sodium concentration. Fig. 6(b) provides the ESEC in the lithium recovery process for all values of Na + /Li + ratios. The change of ESEC in di ff erent Na + /Li + ratios is relatively stable with time. The ESEC had a minimum value (0.032 kWh/mol Li) at Na + /Li + = 1. However, the value of ESEC rises in disparity gradient with the increasing of Na + / Li + , and the largest ESEC value is about 0.85 kWh/mol Li.

texts.orig: The e ff ect of potassium concentration and sodium concentration on lithium migration has a higher similarity. This is root in the similar microstructure of potassium ion and sodium ion. Nevertheless, there should be di ff erent in the e ff ect of potassium and sodium with unlike hydrated radius on lithium migration. So, as one of major coexisting cations in brines, the in fl uence of K + on lithium recovery should not be ignored. In this part work, binary chloride system (Li + , K + //Cl --H2O) with diverse K + /Li + ratios were used as feed solutions to investigate the in fl uence of co-potassium concentration on lithium recovery.

texts.orig: The curves of the Mr of Li + and RLi under di ff erent K + /Li + ratios ranging from 1 to 30 are shown in Fig. 7. It could be observed in Fig. 7(a) that the potassium concentration has a negative in fl uence on the migration rate of Li + . The curve of Mr presents an obvious downward trend with time under K + /Li + = 1. This was due to the ionic strength in desalting compartment at relatively low level, and not enough ions were used to ionic migration with time. This phenomenon led to the current decline and mass transfer force decrease at the later stage of operation. Furthermore, the curves rise before the intermediate stage and then declines at K + /Li + = 3 and 5. This was because the potassium concentration decreased faster than lithium in desalting compartment with the preferential transportation of potassium at the prior stage. It caused the lithium content in desalting compartment was superior in numbers gradually. But mass transfer force decreased at the later stage of operation, which was caused by weaken ionic intensity. What's more, the curves of migration rate are steady with time when the K + /Li + ratios ranges from 10 to 30. That is to say, the migration rate of lithium drops noticeably with the increase of K + /Li + ratio, which led to an extension of transferring time of lithium. Fig. 7(b) indicates that the lithium recovery ratio decreases with increasing of K + /Li + ratio. Higher value of RLi is obtained under K + /Li + = 1 -3 and the values could be achieved to 71.34% and 72.38%, respectively. And the growth rate of above curves is faster before 80 min and then slightly rises. This was mainly due to the large lithium migration rate in the former stage, which resulted in less in fl uence on lithium migration under lower K + / Li + ratio. This also attributed to the rapid decrease of lithium concentration in desalting compartment with time after potassium concentration decreased greatly. In addition, the tendency of RLi curves under K + /Li + = 5 -30 is close each other, and the lower RLi values are obtained when K + /Li + ratio is 20 and 30.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation of lithium and magnesium is a realistic di ffi culty of industrialization of extracting lithium from brines. This becomes more pronounced in the type of brine located in western China with a high Mg 2+ /Li + . Therefore, the concentration of Mg 2+ is a fundamental infl uence factor of separating and purifying lithium in utilization of brines. The Mr of lithium and RLi at di ff erent Mg 2+ /Li + ratios are given in Fig. 10. It can be seen in Fig. 10(a) that the migration rate of lithium declines linearly with time at di ff erent Mg 2+ /Li + ratios. And the slope of curve of lithium migration rate decreases with magnesium increase. The reasons of above-mentioned facts are as follows: Firstly, as the dominant ion in Li + /Mg 2+ system, the lithium ions with lower initial concentration (0.05 mol/L) were rapidly migrated based on the priority selectivity of monovalent ion exchange membrane. Secondly, magnesium ions were accumulated at the interface of solution-membrane in desalting cells in the presence of DC electric fi elds. Selective permeability of the cation exchange membrane should not achieve to 100% for lithium, and a handful of magnesium ions in desalting compartment may replace a certain amount of lithium ions to migrate. And above phenomenon become remarkable with the increase of Mg 2+ /Li + ratio.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 10(b), the recovery ratio of lithium generally decreases with rising addition of magnesium. The results show that the recovery ratio is relatively higher when the Mg 2+ /Li + ratio is 1 or 3, and the corresponding RLi values reach 76.50% and 77.50% after 120 min. Meanwhile, the RLi value is moderate when Mg 2+ /Li + ratio is range from 5 to 15. But the value of RLi decreases to about 60% after 120 min, when the value of Mg 2+ /Li + ratio is 20 (or higher). Besides, all the curves of lithium recovery ratio are in a faster-growing speed before 80 min and then with a slow-growing speed. This should be attributed to the weak competitive of magnesium to lithium, which led to higher migration rate of lithium before halfway of the experiment. Further, lithium concentration in desalting compartment reduces quickly with time, which brings about the stabilization of RLi value at the later stage of the experiment. It should be pointed out that the RLi value under Mg 2+ /Li + = 3 is higher than Mg 2+ /Li + = 1 after 120 min. This is caused by the improvement of the current density and mass transfer driving force with rising addition of magnesium appropriately in a weak ionic strength solution.

Further, lithium concentration in desalting compartment reduces quickly with time, which brings about the stabilization of RLi value at the later stage of the experiment. It should be pointed out that the RLi value under Mg 2+ /Li + = 3 is higher than Mg 2+ /Li + = 1 after 120 min. This is caused by the improvement of the current density and mass transfer driving force with rising addition of magnesium appropriately in a weak ionic strength solution.

texts.text: 3.2.2. Effect of Ca 2+ /Li + mole ratio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The phenomena of concentration polarization exist objectively in ED process, which due to the major di ff erence of ionic transfer numbers between the membrane and the solution [37,38]. If the deletion layer was formed at the desalting side, resulting in not only the rise of the membrane cell voltage, but also splitting of water. Then, some slightly soluble salts (such as Ca(OH)2) adhering to the ion exchange membrane surface can cause and aggravate the concentration polarization. Once the electric current density in membrane surface of desalting cell which is close to cathode compartment was surpassed, water splitting phenomenon would be happened in a chain reaction way. The OH -produced by water splitting process reacts with calcium ions quickly and generates Ca(OH)2. This slightly soluble compound could adhere to the surface of anion exchange membrane, and then led to a sharp rise in ED stack resistance. A substantial increase in energy consumption, scale formation and membrane pore blocking phenomenon would occur in internal ED structure. Further, the lithium ionic transporting quantity falls sharply. This process is also described as ' avalanche reaction ' by Tanaka [37]. Based on above reasons, it is meaningful in practice for investigating the in fl uence of calcium ionic concentration range on lithium recovery, the optimization of energy consumption and the extension of membrane service life.

texts.orig: 3.3. Contrastive analysis of the effect of coexisting cations
texts.text: 3.3. Contrastive analysis of the effect of coexisting cations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As previously mentioned, the cations coexisting with lithium ion in brines contain not only magnesium but also sodium, potassium and calcium. The concentration of these coexisting ions greatly exceeds that of lithium in brines. So, those coexisting cations present competitive migration with lithium, which have unfavorable in fl uence on the enrichment and puri fi cation of Li + . As discussed in Sections 3.1 and 3.2, the obvious correlativity was found between the migration of Li + and the concentration and composition of coexisting cations. In batch S-ED process, the concentrations of various cations decreased at di ff erent rates in desalting compartment. In order to compare the in fl uence of coexisting cations on lithium recovery, and then provide theoretical basis and data support for recovering lithium from brines by S-ED, the experimental data in the initial stage was selected to proceed a contrastive analysis of the e ff ect of coexisting cations on recovering lithium.

For divalent coexisting cations, it is di ffi cult to pass though the monovalent cation exchange membrane owing to its sterically hindered e ff ect, which determines the lower concentration of divalent coexisting cations in membrane phase. Magnesium ion with a bigger hydrated radius relative to calcium ion is in a lower ionic mobility in membrane and a smaller impact on lithium recovery. E ff ect of various coexisting cations on the current e ffi ciency ( η ) and speci fi c energy consumption (ESEC) are shown in Fig. 17. It could be seen that all the curves of η show a downtrend with the M n+ / Li + ratio increase. Furthermore, the curves of η are downhill quickly when M n+ /Li + ratio ranges from 1 to 5. With the concentration of coexisting cations increase, the competitive e ff ect of M n+ presents an enhancement. But there is also a limit in ionic migration amount under a certain applied voltage. So, above enhancement e ff ectiveness is gradually weakened. On the contrary, the curves of ESEC show an uptrend with the increase of M n+ /Li + . In short, the e ff ect of monovalent cations (Na + , K + ) are much stronger than divalent cations (Mg 2+ , Ca 2+ ) on current e ffi ciency and speci fi c energy consumption. And the in fl uence follows the sequence: K + >Na + >Ca 2+ > Mg 2+ , which is consistent with the e ff ect on migration rate and recovery ratio of lithium.

texts.orig: 3.4.1. The presence modality of cations in solution
texts.text: 3.4.1. The presence modality of cations in solution

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is well known that the cations in solution exist in the form of hydrated ions. So there is an interaction-space of water molecules around the cations. The structure of water molecules could be destroyed when the hydration free energy between cations and water molecules was greater than the hydrogen bonds energy between water molecules. Then the hydrated fi lm formed around the cations. There are two layers in the hydration fi lm: the fi rst hydration layer and the second one. And they can be partially damaged by electrostatic forces and thus become free water molecules. Meanwhile, the second hydration layer is comparatively more easily damaged than the fi rst one which is closer to and combined more fi rmly with the cations. Besides, a thicker hydration layer formed around the ions having smaller ionic radius and bigger charge number. Those water molecules combined with cations tightly should lose the ability of independent movement. Thus, the ionic hydration caused the decrease of the free water molecules number in solution and the increase of ionic space volumes.

texts.orig: that the membrane is selective for certain ions such as monovalent cation/anion. Selective permeation mechanism of monovalent cation exchange membrane could be made up of pore action of the membrane and electrostatic interaction. The pore action could attribute to its structure seen as bent ion channels inside the ion exchange membrane. The ionic migration from one side of ion exchange membrane to another side is achieved under this action. Just as ionic sieve used as solid phase adsorbent has a sieve e ff ect origin in its uniform microporous structure [42], the pore diameter of ion exchange membrane for desalination also belongs to nanoscale mostly, and then has selected separation for the ions with di ff erent volume based on above ' sieve effect ' . The intensity of this ' sieve e ff ect ' should be controlled by the sizes and uniformity of pores. And the ions passing through the membrane might occur when the ionic hydrated radius is less than the average pore radius. Another reason is that the thin layer with positive charge is deposited in the surface of monovalent selective cation exchange membrane by electrodepositing or bonding method. Compared with the monovalent cation which has a larger hydrated ion radius, the multivalent cation with a smaller hydrated ion radius is su ff ered strongly from electrostatic repulsion of the membrane surface. So, the ion exchange reaction between multivalent cation and monovalent selective cation exchange membrane with positive charge in electrodialysis process is more di ffi cult.

Selective permeation mechanism of monovalent cation exchange membrane could be made up of pore action of the membrane and electrostatic interaction. The pore action could attribute to its structure seen as bent ion channels inside the ion exchange membrane. The ionic migration from one side of ion exchange membrane to another side is achieved under this action. Just as ionic sieve used as solid phase adsorbent has a sieve e ff ect origin in its uniform microporous structure [42], the pore diameter of ion exchange membrane for desalination also belongs to nanoscale mostly, and then has selected separation for the ions with di ff erent volume based on above ' sieve effect ' . The intensity of this ' sieve e ff ect ' should be controlled by the sizes and uniformity of pores. And the ions passing through the membrane might occur when the ionic hydrated radius is less than the average pore radius. Another reason is that the thin layer with positive charge is deposited in the surface of monovalent selective cation exchange membrane by electrodepositing or bonding method. Compared with the monovalent cation which has a larger hydrated ion radius, the multivalent cation with a smaller hydrated ion radius is su ff ered strongly from electrostatic repulsion of the membrane surface. So, the ion exchange reaction between multivalent cation and monovalent selective cation exchange membrane with positive charge in electrodialysis process is more di ffi cult.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Essentially, ED process could be considered as a continuous ion exchange process. It was reasonable to explain the migration of ions throughout the membrane with a selective ion exchange process. Some researches about the selectivity of some ions have been made and many interpretations have been proposed. Based on the coulomb interaction, Mukherjee and Sengupta [43] indicated that hydrated ionic radius played a decisive role in the selectivity of di ff erent ions. Therein, a dehydration theory was put forward by Eisenman [44] to account for the selective sequence of some ions with known hydrated radiuses. In this theory, a free energy of ion exchange is controlled by the electrostatic e ff ect deducting the interference of water in the ion-surface. Nevertheless, there is some inadequacy for applying this theory to ED process because of the existence of water molecular migration [45,46]. Afterwards, considering the Eisenman dehydration theory could give a reasonable explanation for the selective ion exchange order, a conceptual model of partial dehydration was put forward to analyze the e ff ect order of coexisting cations (Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Ca 2+ ) on lithium migration in S-ED process from microcosmic point of view.

texts.orig: The partial dehydration mechanism is based on two aspects: charge capillary column theory and ionic potential. Generally, ionic migration was carried out with the mode of shortest path and least resistance in electro-membrane process. Ohtaki and Radnai [47] introduced a model of ionic migration in a charged capillary column to simplify the migration. Considering the ions transport via the adjacent functional site of ion exchange membrane, the diameter of capillary was hypothetically similar to the space between adjacent functional sites. Ionic potential is the ratio of ionic charge (Z) and ionic radius (r), i.e., Z/r or Z 2 / r. It was usually used to explain how strongly an ion electrostatically attracted to the counter-ion. Similarly, hydration potential which is associated with Z 2 /r value represents how strongly an ion will attract water molecules [48,49]. Therefore, smaller value of Z 2 /r means smaller hydrated ionic radius. The relationship of Z 2 /r value and hydration free energy of some cations is shown in Fig. 18. It could be seen that the hydrated ionic free energy is positively correlated with Z 2 /r. Generally, ionic electro-migration in ED process needs to spend some energy. texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 19, the transmembrane migration process of M n+ is described visually by a partial dehydration behavior. A shear stress could be generated when M n+ passes through the capillary under a certain potential di ff erence. In the early stage, the M n+ might temporarily reduce its hydration number through removing some water molecules, which is derived from the second hydration shells mostly, and then enters the interior region of the membrane. After the transmembrane process, the M n+ is migrated to concentrating compartment and recombined with some free water molecules. Then, the electromigration process of M n+ from desalting cell to concentrating cell is completed. And, smaller Z 2 /r value (or smaller hydration free energy) of the ions represents that it is more easily for their dehydration and a higher selectivity.

texts.orig: Based on the above description, the explanation of the e ff ect sequence of coexisting cations (Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Ca 2+ ) on lithium migration in this study is derived as follows. As shown in Fig. 18, the values of Z 2 /r of monovalent cations (like Na + and K + ) are obvious smaller than divalent cations (like Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ), which is consistent with the ability of attracting free water molecules of ionic center charge by electrostatic forces, results in the stronger negative e ff ect of monovalent cations on lithium migration. Videlicet, higher Z 2 /r like Ca 2+ and Mg 2+ (in Fig. 18) should need higher energy to carry out corresponding partial dehydrated process. Furthermore, the Z 2 /r value of K + is little smaller than Na + , which leads to the much in fl uence of K + on lithium migration. Even though the Z 2 /r value of coexisting monovalent cations are similar with Li + , it is obvious that the great in fl uences of Na + and K + on lithium migration are observed in actual S-ED process because there are plenty of Na + and K + in nature brines. Besides, the Z 2 /r value of Ca 2+ is slight smaller than Mg 2+ , which causes the e ff ect of Ca 2+ slightly big somewhat. To sum up, the in fl uence sequence of coexisting cations with the same concentration on lithium migration is K + >Na + >Ca 2+ > Mg 2+ .
